does: This paper introduces DV-World, a comprehensive benchmark of 260 tasks for evaluating data visualization agents in realistic professional scenarios. The benchmark spans three distinct domains: DV-Sheet for native spreadsheet operations including chart creation and dashboard repair, DV-Evolution for adapting visualizations across different programming paradigms and data contexts, and DV-Interact for handling ambiguous user requirements through interaction with a simulated user. The work proposes a hybrid evaluation framework combining Table-value Alignment for numerical accuracy assessment with multimodal LLM-as-a-Judge using rubrics for semantic and visual quality evaluation. Experiments with state-of-the-art models demonstrate performance below 50% overall, revealing significant gaps in agents' abilities to handle complex, real-world data visualization tasks that require environmental grounding, cross-platform adaptation, and intent disambiguation.
connects: This work addresses limitations in existing data visualization benchmarks that operate in isolated code-sandbox environments and focus narrowly on visualization creation from scratch. It builds on prior research in vision-language models for visual understanding, agent-based task automation, and benchmarking methodologies for evaluating AI systems on complex tasks. The paper extends frameworks for intent alignment and interactive learning from dialogue systems and collaborative AI work, while drawing on evaluation approaches from visual question answering and multimodal assessment. It responds to growing recognition that realistic evaluation requires systems to handle multiple programming paradigms, work with imperfect specifications, and operate within real software ecosystems rather than simplified isolated environments.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into more robust data visualization agents capable of handling professional workflows where requirements are ambiguous and visualization artifacts must evolve with new data. The three-domain structure and user interaction framework establish new evaluation paradigms for assessing proactive intent alignment in agent systems beyond visualization. The hybrid evaluation methodology combining numerical and semantic assessment could inform broader approaches to evaluating multimodal AI on structured tasks. By exposing critical performance gaps in current models, the work points toward research in grounded reasoning, cross-platform system interaction, and dialogue-based refinement for visualization agents. It also creates a foundation for studying how agents can learn from user feedback and adapt visualizations incrementally rather than treating visualization as a single-shot creation problem.
